passages.explanation: We who are strong ought to bear with the failings of the weak and not to please ourselves. Each of us should please our neighbours for their good, to build them up. For even Christ did not please himself — as it is written: the insults of those who insult you have fallen on me. Everything written in the past was written to teach us, so that through the endurance taught in the Scriptures and the encouragement they provide we might have hope. May the God who gives endurance and encouragement give you the same attitude of mind toward each other that Christ Jesus had, so that with one mind and one voice you may glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. Paul's argument reaches its conclusion: the strong should carry the weak, not look down on them. The model is Christ himself, who absorbed insults rather than insisting on his rights. Unity isn't uniformity — it's a shared voice of worship despite different convictions on secondary matters.

Accept one another, then, just as Christ accepted you, in order to bring praise to God. For I tell you that Christ has become a servant of the Jews on behalf of God's truth, so that the promises made to the patriarchs might be confirmed and, moreover, that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. Paul then strings together four Old Testament quotations — from 2 Samuel, Deuteronomy, Psalm 117, and Isaiah 11 — all showing that God always planned for the Gentiles to praise him alongside Israel. May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace as you trust in him, so that you may overflow with hope by the power of the Holy Spirit. The four Scripture citations are a barrage of evidence: God's plan for the Gentiles isn't an afterthought — it runs through the Law, the Prophets, and the Psalms. The prayer that closes this section is one of Paul's richest: joy, peace, trust, overflowing hope, all powered by the Spirit.

Paul tells the Romans he's convinced they're full of goodness, filled with knowledge, and competent to instruct one another. Yet he's written boldly on some points as a reminder, because of the grace God gave him to be a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles. He serves as a priest of God's gospel so that the Gentiles might become an offering acceptable to God, sanctified by the Holy Spirit. He glories in Christ Jesus in his service to God. He won't venture to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through him in leading the Gentiles to obey God by what he's said and done — by the power of signs and wonders, through the power of the Spirit of God. From Jerusalem all the way around to Illyricum, he has fully proclaimed the gospel of Christ. He's always aimed to preach the gospel where Christ was not known, so he wouldn't be building on someone else's foundation. As Isaiah wrote: those who were not told about him will see, and those who have not heard will understand. This is why he's been hindered from coming to them. Paul defines his mission: pioneer preaching in unreached areas. He quotes Isaiah 52:15 to justify this strategy. He hasn't come to Rome because Rome already has a church — and Paul's calling is to go where no one has gone.

But now, since there's no more place for him to work in these regions and since he's been longing for many years to visit them, he plans to do so when he goes to Spain. He hopes to see them while passing through and to have them assist him on his journey there, after he's enjoyed their company for a while. But first he must go to Jerusalem to serve the Lord's people there. Macedonia and Achaia were pleased to make a contribution for the poor among the saints in Jerusalem. They were pleased to do it, and indeed they owe it to them — if the Gentiles have shared in the Jews' spiritual blessings, they owe it to the Jews to share with them their material blessings. After he's completed this task and delivered the funds safely, he'll go to Spain and visit them on the way. He knows that when he comes, he'll come in the full measure of the blessing of Christ. He urges them to join him in his struggle by praying for him — that he may be kept safe from the unbelievers in Judea, that his contribution to Jerusalem will be favourably received, and that by God's will he may come to them with joy and be refreshed in their company. The God of peace be with you all. Amen. Paul's travel plans reveal his strategy: Jerusalem first (delivering the Gentile collection), then Rome (as a staging ground), then Spain (the western edge of the known world). He asks for prayer about Jerusalem because he knows it's dangerous — and Acts shows he was right. The collection from Gentile churches for Jewish believers is itself a theological statement: the gospel creates material solidarity across ethnic lines.
